titleStmt.title: Letter from William Tyrrell Jr. to William Tyrrell Sr., 27 December 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Tyrrell Jr.

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
The letters of William Upton Tyrrell ('Willie') to his father William J.H. Tyrrell, mother Elizabeth, sister Minnie and brother Robert are a notable example of wartime correspondence. The letters - nearly 200 in total - are mostly written by William to his family while in active service with the Royal Irish Rifles in France. Willie discusses his life and the lives of other soldiers in the trenches, as well as life at Ballindoolin, the family estate.
The family name 'Tyrrell' is sometimes misspelled 'Tyrell' in correspondence to the family.
The present letter is sent from the Lewis Gun School. Willie received a parcel on the 24th and a letter on the 25th. He has accumulated so much kit that it takes two men to carry it. He had goose for Christmas dinner and sampled the soldiers rationed plum pudding which he found satisfactory.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
27/12/17 My dear Daddy, Here I am at the Lewis Gun School and
living in the height of luxury. The weather is still
cold with deep snow on the grounds and it is again
snowing. I will be here until 4th Jan. so you
need not change the address on my letters.
I will be back again before they would be
here. The cakes were absolutely delicious
and so was every thing in those three
splendid parcels which came just the day
before xmas. Mamy's letter enclosing
Mr Boohey's card arrived on Xmas day. There is really not a word of news.
We had a very quiet and very happy
Xmas day but unfortunately it snowed
all day. We were to have a batallion
dinner but it could not be arranged
so we just had our own company messes.
We procured an excellent goose. The men
all got splendid dinners too. The ration
plum pudding was as very good. We had
some of it too. My kit has now reached such an
enormous size that it takes two men to
carry it. I will really have to send a lot
of stuff home as our allowance is 35 lbs. I have a quantity of stuff which I do not
require so i will get it away at the first
opportunity. I am not as near C. Davison as I thought
I would be so I doubt whether I will see him.
It would mean a train journey and I
am not sure that I could get away. I will write you tomorrow when I know
more about this place. I only got here at 11 last
night. With very best love to all Your most affec Son
Willie
